questions.question: Which IETF working group is responsible for PQC in X.509 certificates?
questions.explanation: LAMPS (Limited Additional Mechanisms for PKIX and SMIME) is the IETF working group handling PQC integration in X.509 certificates, CMS, and S/MIME. They are developing drafts for ML-DSA certificates and composite signatures.

questions.question: What does draft-ietf-tls-hybrid-design define?

options.text: Hybrid key exchange combining classical ECDH with ML-KEM

questions.explanation: The TLS hybrid design draft defines how to combine classical key exchange (like X25519) with post-quantum key exchange (ML-KEM) in TLS 1.3, providing security against both classical and quantum attacks during the transition period.

questions.question: When is PCI DSS expected to require PQC?

options.text: Never, PCI only covers physical security

questions.explanation: PCI SSC is monitoring PQC developments and is expected to issue requirements in the 2026-2028 timeframe. Organizations should begin PQC readiness assessments now to meet future requirements.

questions.question: Which RFC defines ML-KEM usage in IPsec/IKEv2?
questions.explanation: RFC 9370 defines how to use ML-KEM for post-quantum key exchange in IKEv2, enabling quantum-resistant VPN connections. This was one of the first RFCs to incorporate NIST PQC algorithms.

questions.question: What is 3GPP's current status on PQC for 5G?

options.text: PQC feasibility study complete, integration in future releases
3GPP is not involved in cryptography standards

questions.explanation: 3GPP's SA3 security working group has completed PQC feasibility studies and is working on integration for future releases (Release 18+). Full PQC support will come through 5G and 6G specification updates.

questions.question: What is the PQUIP IETF working group focused on?
questions.explanation: PQUIP (Post-Quantum Use In Protocols) is an IETF working group focused on providing guidance for using post-quantum algorithms in various Internet protocols, addressing practical integration challenges.

questions.question: Healthcare organizations using FHIR should ensure their implementations support PQC-capable TLS.
questions.explanation: HL7 FHIR uses TLS for transport security. As TLS standards incorporate PQC (via hybrid key exchange and PQC cipher suites), healthcare organizations should ensure their FHIR implementations can support these updates.
